items.title: Understanding Bacterial Meningitis: Definition and Epidemiology
items.content: Bacterial meningitis is defined as an acute purulent infection within the subarachnoid space, often involving CNS inflammation. It has significant morbidity due to complications like decreased consciousness and raised ICP.
keyPoints: Annual incidence in the US: ~1.4 cases/100,000 population
Common causative organisms: Streptococcus pneumoniae (~50%), Neisseria meningitidis (~25%)

items.title: Etiology and Risk Factors of Bacterial Meningitis
items.content: Streptococcus pneumoniae is the leading cause in adults >20 years, with risk factors including pneumococcal pneumonia and alcoholism. Vaccination has reduced N. meningitidis incidence.
keyPoints: S. pneumoniae: most common cause in adults >20 years
Annual cases of S. pneumoniae meningitis: 1.1 per 100,000 persons
Vaccines reduce N. meningitidis incidence; risk increased with complement inhibitors

items.question: Which organism is most commonly responsible for community-acquired bacterial meningitis in the United States?
items.explanation: Streptococcus pneumoniae accounts for approximately 50% of community-acquired bacterial meningitis cases.

items.question: What is the approximate annual incidence of bacterial meningitis in the United States?
items.explanation: The annual incidence of bacterial meningitis in the United States is approximately 1.4 cases per 100,000 population.

items.question: Which condition is a major risk factor for pneumococcal meningitis?
items.explanation: Pneumococcal pneumonia is a major predisposing condition that increases the risk of pneumococcal meningitis.

items.statement: N. meningitidis causes recurring epidemics of meningitis every 8–12 years.
items.explanation: Neisseria meningitidis is known to cause recurring epidemics of meningitis approximately every 8–12 years.

items.statement: The serogroup B meningococcal vaccines significantly reduce nasopharyngeal carriage of meningococci.
items.explanation: Serogroup B meningococcal vaccines do not significantly reduce nasopharyngeal carriage, which is the major source of person-to-person transmission.
